rationale_for_baseline_tier: A reproductive-age woman with ~6 weeks amenorrhoea, sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, vaginal bleeding, shoulder-tip pain and presyncope with pallor/diaphoresis has a leaking/ruptured ectopic pregnancy with haemoperitoneum until proven otherwise. This is life-threatening concealed internal haemorrhage that can decompensate within minutes; the only appropriate disposition is an emergency (Priority 1) ambulance — T5 — with the patient kept flat, legs elevated and nil by mouth. Waiting for a GP or self-transport risks catastrophic haemorrhagic shock and death.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening pain, increasing faintness, or collapse before the ambulance arrives
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call 000 back and keep the line open
escalation_edges.rationale: Progressive symptoms indicate ongoing bleeding and impending shock; the ambulance response must be expedited.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If her pain gets worse or she feels more faint before the ambulance arrives, call 000 straight back, keep her lying flat with her legs raised, and tell them she is deteriorating.

escalation_edges.rationale: Collapse signals severe blood loss; positioning and, if she stops breathing normally, CPR are the critical interventions while paramedics are en route.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she becomes unresponsive, lay her flat on the floor, raise her legs higher, and tell 000 immediately — they will guide you, and start CPR if she stops breathing normally.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden heavy vaginal bleeding or feeling she is about to pass out
escalation_edges.rationale: Marks acute deterioration; reinforces the emergency ambulance disposition.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the bleeding suddenly becomes heavy or she feels she's going to pass out, that's an emergency — make sure 000 knows and keep her flat with legs raised.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: The partner is present but highly distressed
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give the partner simple, concrete instructions (call 000, keep her flat with legs raised, nothing to eat or drink, unlock the front door, watch her breathing) and stay reassuring — a panicked carer needs clear direction to act effectively.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and say she has sudden severe lower-belly pain, is faint, and her period is late so she could be pregnant. Keep her lying flat with her legs raised. Do not let her stand, walk, eat or drink. Do not drive her yourselves — wait for the ambulance. Unlock the front door and stay with her.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours or a GP appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Handover to the emergency department and gynaecology/surgical team; the hospital will provide a discharge summary after diagnosis and treatment for GP follow-up.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as a routine or same-day GP problem, advising simple analgesia, or arranging outpatient work-up instead of an emergency ambulance.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Ongoing concealed haemorrhage progresses to hypovolaemic shock and death during the delay to definitive surgery.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Directing the patient to attend the ED by her own/partner's transport rather than by emergency ambulance — she could collapse en route without monitoring or resuscitation capability.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: T5 is the correct tier here, so there is effectively no meaningful over-triage; escalating any reproductive-age woman with acute pelvic pain and syncope is appropriately conservative.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot confirm pregnancy, measure vitals, examine the abdomen or image the pelvis, so the diagnosis cannot be established remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the constellation as a possible ruptured ectopic / life-threatening bleeding and escalate to 000; explain that it cannot be safely excluded from a distance.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Using the inability to confirm as a reason to downgrade urgency, reassure, or defer to a GP appointment.
